beginner.question: What is a CSS combinator?
beginner.answer: A CSS combinator defines the relationship between two or more selectors, determining how elements are selected based on their relationship in the DOM.

beginner.question: What does the descendant combinator (space) do?
beginner.answer: The descendant combinator (a space) selects elements that are descendants (children, grandchildren, etc.) of a specified element.

beginner.question: What does the child combinator (>) do?
beginner.answer: The child combinator (>) selects direct children only — elements that are immediate children of the specified parent.

beginner.question: What does the adjacent sibling combinator (+) do?
beginner.answer: The adjacent sibling combinator (+) selects an element that is immediately preceded by a specified sibling.

beginner.question: What does the general sibling combinator (~) do?
beginner.answer: The general sibling combinator (~) selects all elements that are preceded by a specified sibling, not just the immediate one.

beginner.question: What is the syntax for the descendant combinator?
beginner.answer: The syntax is ancestor descendant { ... }, e.g., div p selects all p elements inside a div.

beginner.question: What is the syntax for the child combinator?
beginner.answer: The syntax is parent > child { ... }, e.g., ul > li selects all li elements that are direct children of a ul.

beginner.question: Does the descendant combinator select grandchildren?
beginner.answer: Yes, the descendant combinator selects all nested elements at any depth, not just direct children.

beginner.question: Does the child combinator select grandchildren?
beginner.answer: No, the child combinator only selects direct children of the specified parent.

beginner.question: What is the difference between div p and div > p?
beginner.answer: div p selects all p elements inside div at any depth; div > p selects only p elements that are direct children of div.

intermediate.question: How does selector specificity work with combinators?
intermediate.answer: Combinators themselves have no specificity value; specificity is determined by the combined selectors (IDs, classes, elements) they connect.

intermediate.question: What does h2 + p select?
intermediate.answer: h2 + p selects the first paragraph that immediately follows an h2 element (adjacent sibling).

intermediate.question: What does h2 ~ p select?
intermediate.answer: h2 ~ p selects all paragraph elements that follow an h2 element and share the same parent.

intermediate.question: Can you chain multiple combinators in one selector?
intermediate.answer: Yes, you can chain combinators, e.g., div > ul > li + li selects the second li onward directly inside a ul that is a direct child of div.

intermediate.question: What is the difference between adjacent and general sibling combinators?
intermediate.answer: Adjacent (+) selects only the immediate next sibling; general (~) selects all subsequent siblings.

intermediate.question: How do combinators affect CSS performance?
intermediate.answer: Combinators affect how the browser parses selectors; deeply nested combinators can slow down style matching on large DOMs.

intermediate.question: Can combinators be used with pseudo-classes?
intermediate.answer: Yes, combinators work with pseudo-classes, e.g., div > p:first-child selects the first paragraph that is a direct child of a div.

intermediate.question: What does ul li.active > a select?
intermediate.answer: It selects anchor elements that are direct children of list items with class active that are descendants of a ul.

intermediate.question: How do you select an element that follows another element with a specific class?
intermediate.answer: Use .class + element or .class ~ element, e.g., .warning + p selects the paragraph immediately after an element with class warning.

intermediate.question: What is the specificity of div > .class?
intermediate.answer: div > .class has a specificity of (0,1,1) — 0 IDs, 1 class, 1 element.

advanced.question: How does the browser evaluate selectors with combinators from right to left?
advanced.answer: Browsers read selectors right-to-left, matching the key selector first, then filtering ancestors/siblings — improving performance by reducing candidate checks.

advanced.question: What are the limitations of the sibling combinators in terms of DOM traversal?
advanced.answer: Sibling combinators only select elements that come after the initial element; they cannot select preceding siblings.

advanced.question: How do combinators interact with the :has() pseudo-class?
advanced.answer: :has() allows parent and previous sibling selection (e.g., div:has(p) selects a div containing a p), extending combinator reach.

advanced.question: What is the difference between div :first-child and div:first-child?
advanced.answer: div :first-child (with space) selects any first-child inside a div; div:first-child selects a div that is itself a first child of its parent.

advanced.question: How does specificity interact with the :not() pseudo-class using combinators?
advanced.answer: The argument inside :not() has full specificity weight, but :not itself has none. Complex selectors inside :not() with combinators are now supported.

advanced.question: What happens when combinators are combined with compound selectors incorrectly?
advanced.answer: Incorrect combination (e.g., div>.class without spaces) can still work but is brittle; missing spaces change the meaning entirely.

advanced.question: How do logical combinator chains work with :is() and :where()?
advanced.answer: :is() and :where() accept selector lists including combinators, e.g., :is(div > p, span > p), but specificity is calculated from the most specific argument.

advanced.question: Can you nest combinators inside @scope for scoped styles?
advanced.answer: Yes, @scope uses a scoping root and lower bound, effectively creating dynamic combinator-like boundaries within a stylesheet.

advanced.question: How does the :nth-child() pseudo-class relate to combinator selection?
advanced.answer: :nth-child(an+b) selects by position among siblings; combined with combinators, you can target specific items in complex structures.

advanced.question: What is the performance impact of using the general sibling combinator with many elements?
advanced.answer: The general sibling selector can match many elements after the reference element, potentially slowing style calculation in large DOM trees.
